learning_objectives: Trace the intellectual origins of human rights from natural law to contemporary frameworks
Evaluate the universalism vs. relativism debate
Analyze different generations of human rights and their political implications
readings: Donnelly, Universal Human Rights in Theory and Practice Ch. 1-3
Mutua, 'Savages, Victims, and Saviors: The Metaphor of Human Rights'

lecture_notes.introduction: Human rights are among the most powerful ideas in modern politics, yet their philosophical foundations are deeply contested. Understanding these debates is essential for effective advocacy.
key_concepts: Natural rights tradition: Locke, Rousseau, the Enlightenment
Three generations: civil-political, economic-social-cultural, collective-solidarity
Universalism: rights inhere in all humans by virtue of their humanity
Cultural relativism: rights must be understood within cultural contexts
Postcolonial critiques: human rights as Western imperialism?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Structured debate: are human rights truly universal or culturally constructed?
setup_requirements: Debate preparation materials for both positions
Primary source excerpts from diverse philosophical traditions

review.value: First: civil and political (liberty rights); Second: economic, social, and cultural (equality rights); Third: collective/solidarity rights (development, environment, peace)
The debate over whether human rights apply identically everywhere (universalism) or must be adapted to local cultural contexts (relativism), with significant political stakes

learning_objectives: Map the institutional architecture of international human rights protection
Analyze the strengths and weaknesses of treaty-based monitoring
Evaluate the UN Human Rights Council and Universal Periodic Review
readings: UN Human Rights Council resolution establishing UPR (A/RES/60/251)

lecture_notes.introduction: The international human rights regime has expanded dramatically since 1948, but enforcement remains its greatest challenge.
key_concepts: Core human rights treaties: ICCPR, ICESCR, CEDAW, CRC, CRPD, CAT, CERD, CED, CMW
Treaty bodies: monitoring, reporting, individual complaints
UN Human Rights Council: membership controversies and politicization
Universal Periodic Review: peer assessment of all UN members
Special Procedures: Special Rapporteurs and Working Groups
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a real UPR report: identifying recommendations, responses, and implementation gaps

review.value: The UN process through which every member state's human rights record is reviewed by peer states every 4.5 years, combining universality with political accountability
Committees of independent experts that monitor implementation of core human rights treaties through state reports, individual complaints, and general comments

learning_objectives: Define genocide under the Genocide Convention and analyze its legal elements
Evaluate early warning indicators and prevention frameworks
Assess the R2P framework's effectiveness in preventing atrocities
readings: Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide (1948) Articles I-V
UN Framework of Analysis for Atrocity Crimes (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: The promise of 'never again' has been broken repeatedly. Understanding genocide and developing effective prevention strategies is a moral and practical imperative.
key_concepts: Genocide Convention: definition, intent requirement, protected groups
Stages of genocide: Stanton's ten stages model
Early warning: risk factors, indicators, and institutional mechanisms
R2P's three pillars and the debate over military intervention
Case studies: Rwanda (failure), Libya (controversial success), Syria (failure)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Early warning exercise: assessing genocide risk indicators for a fictional country scenario

review.value: Acts committed with intent to destroy, in whole or in part, a national, ethnical, racial, or religious group; the specific intent (dolus specialis) requirement makes prosecution challenging
Stanton's model: classification, symbolization, discrimination, dehumanization, organization, polarization, preparation, persecution, extermination, denial

class_schedule.topic: Transitional Justice: Trials, Truth, and Reconciliation
learning_objectives: Compare different transitional justice mechanisms: trials, truth commissions, reparations, lustration
Evaluate the trade-offs between justice and peace in post-conflict societies
Analyze the ICC's role in transitional justice

lecture_notes.introduction: How should societies deal with legacies of mass atrocity? The field of transitional justice offers multiple approaches, each with strengths and trade-offs.
key_concepts: Criminal prosecution: Nuremberg, ICTY, ICTR, ICC
Truth commissions: South Africa TRC, Colombia, and others
Reparations: individual and collective, material and symbolic
Lustration and institutional reform: vetting perpetrators from public office
Justice vs. peace: the amnesty debate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Policy design exercise: designing a transitional justice program for a fictional post-conflict country

review.value: A temporary body established to investigate a pattern of human rights abuses, acknowledge victims, and produce a public record; may or may not include amnesty provisions
The tension between pursuing accountability for past crimes (which may destabilize fragile peace) and prioritizing stability and reconciliation (which may mean impunity for perpetrators)

class_schedule.topic: Contemporary Human Rights Challenges: Technology, Migration, and Democracy
learning_objectives: Analyze human rights implications of digital surveillance and AI
Evaluate the rights of refugees and migrants under international law
Assess the impact of democratic backsliding on human rights protection
readings: Zuboff, The Age of Surveillance Capitalism Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Human rights face new challenges in the 21st century: mass surveillance, forced displacement on an unprecedented scale, and the erosion of democratic governance worldwide.
key_concepts: Digital rights: privacy, surveillance, data protection
AI and human rights: algorithmic bias, autonomous weapons, facial recognition
Refugee rights: non-refoulement, asylum, and the global compact on refugees
Climate displacement: rights of climate refugees
Democratic backsliding: shrinking civic space and authoritarian resilience
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case analysis: evaluating the human rights implications of China's social credit system

review.value: The core principle of refugee law prohibiting states from returning refugees to countries where they face persecution; considered a jus cogens norm
The global trend of governments restricting NGOs, media, and civil society through legal, administrative, and extra-legal means, undermining human rights monitoring and advocacy

class_schedule.topic: Human Rights Advocacy: Strategy, Tools, and Ethics
learning_objectives: Analyze the strategies and tactics of human rights organizations
Evaluate the effectiveness of naming and shaming, litigation, and grassroots mobilization
Address ethical dilemmas in human rights advocacy
readings: Keck & Sikkink, Activists Beyond Borders Ch. 3 (excerpt)
Hopgood, The Endtimes of Human Rights Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Human rights do not protect themselves. Effective advocacy requires strategy, coalition-building, and confronting difficult ethical choices.
key_concepts: Naming and shaming: when does it work?
Strategic litigation in regional and international courts
Grassroots mobilization and community-based approaches
Business and human rights: the UN Guiding Principles
Ethical dilemmas: prioritization, instrumentalization, savior narratives
lecture_notes.demonstration: Advocacy campaign design: developing a human rights campaign strategy for a selected issue

review.value: The strategy of publicly identifying and criticizing human rights violators to mobilize pressure; most effective against states that care about their international reputation

review.key: UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights
review.value: The 2011 framework establishing state duty to protect, corporate responsibility to respect, and access to remedy for business-related human rights harms

quizzes.topic: Human Rights Foundations and the International Regime

questions.question: Explain the distinction between universalism and cultural relativism in human rights. What is the strongest argument for each position, and how might they be reconciled?
questions.answer: Universalism holds that human rights apply to all people by virtue of their humanity, regardless of culture, and that certain rights (like freedom from torture) permit no cultural exception. Cultural relativism argues that rights are culturally constructed and that imposing Western-origin standards on non-Western societies is a form of cultural imperialism. The strongest argument for universalism is that certain rights (protection from genocide, torture, slavery) are so fundamental that no cultural justification can override them. The strongest argument for relativism is that specific implementations of rights (e.g., family law, gender norms) legitimately vary and that external imposition undermines local ownership. Reconciliation may come through cross-cultural dialogue that identifies overlapping consensus on core rights while allowing variation in implementation.

questions.question: What is the Universal Periodic Review and why was it created? Identify one strength and one weakness of the UPR process.
questions.answer: The UPR is a UN Human Rights Council mechanism through which every UN member state's human rights record is reviewed by peer states on a cyclical basis (every 4.5 years). It was created to address the politicization and selectivity of the former UN Commission on Human Rights, which focused criticism on some states while ignoring others. Strength: universality -- every state is reviewed under the same process, eliminating the perception of selective targeting. Weakness: the process relies on peer review by states that may have their own poor records, leading to soft recommendations and mutual restraint ('you don't criticize me, I won't criticize you'); implementation of recommendations is voluntary and often poor.

quizzes.topic: Genocide, Transitional Justice, and Contemporary Challenges

questions.question: What are the key elements of the crime of genocide under the 1948 Genocide Convention? Why is the 'intent' requirement particularly challenging for prosecution?
questions.answer: The Genocide Convention defines genocide as acts committed with the intent to destroy, in whole or in part, a national, ethnical, racial, or religious group. The prohibited acts include killing, causing serious harm, imposing conditions calculated to destroy, preventing births, and forcibly transferring children. The intent requirement (dolus specialis) is challenging because prosecutors must prove specific intent to destroy a group as such, not merely intent to commit the individual acts. Perpetrators rarely state their genocidal intent explicitly; it must be inferred from patterns of conduct, statements, scale of atrocities, and systematic targeting. This high evidentiary bar means that mass atrocities may constitute crimes against humanity but fail to meet the genocide threshold, as seen in debates over Darfur and the Uyghur situation.

questions.question: Describe the principle of non-refoulement in refugee law. Under what circumstances, if any, can a state lawfully return a person to a country where they face persecution?
questions.answer: Non-refoulement is the principle that states must not return (refouler) refugees or asylum seekers to a country where they face persecution. It is the cornerstone of the 1951 Refugee Convention (Article 33) and is widely considered a norm of customary international law, possibly jus cogens. The Refugee Convention allows one narrow exception: a state may return a refugee if there are reasonable grounds for regarding them as a danger to national security or if they have been convicted of a particularly serious crime and constitute a danger to the community (Art. 33(2)). However, under international human rights law (CAT, ICCPR), the prohibition on return to face torture is absolute with no exceptions, creating a higher standard that overrides the Convention exception when torture is at risk.

rubric.key: Notes the absolute prohibition on return to torture

major_assignments.title: Human Rights Country Report and Advocacy Strategy
major_assignments.description: Select a country and produce a comprehensive human rights assessment modeled on professional reports (Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, State Department). Then develop an evidence-based advocacy strategy to address the most critical issues identified.
objectives: Conduct rigorous human rights research and documentation
Apply international legal standards to assess a country's record
Develop a strategic advocacy campaign with realistic targets

criteria: Comprehensive coverage of civil, political, economic, and social rights
Accurate application of international legal standards
Evidence from credible sources (UN, NGOs, media, government)
Professional report format and balanced tone
Strategy addresses priority issues from the report
Targets and tactics are specific and realistic
Multiple pressure points and tools identified
Ethical considerations addressed
Diverse and credible sources used
Primary sources (UN documents, laws) engaged
Stakeholder mapping is thorough and accurate
Proper citation throughout
Clear and compelling presentation of findings
Advocacy strategy persuasively argued
Effective visual aids
Strong response to questions
Professional-quality human rights country assessment
Strategic and evidence-based advocacy plan
Strong research with diverse credible sources
Effective presentation and communication

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on human rights law, institutions, and case studies

participation.description: Engagement in debates, moot exercises, and policy design activities

major_assignments.description: Human rights country report and advocacy strategy

midterm_examination.description: Exam on human rights theory, law, and institutions

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam with case analysis and policy application

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance required. More than 3 unexcused absences reduce participation grade by 50%.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late papers penalized 5% per day. Advocacy memos cannot be submitted late.
course_policies.collaboration: Debate preparation is collaborative. All written analysis individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Proper citation required. Misrepresenting human rights data results in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to UN human rights databases, Amnesty and HRW reports, and academic journals.
